summary.applicability_rationale: RFC 8826 defines the WebRTC threat model and security requirements that directly apply to WebRTC applications. Unlike the transport-level protocols that are implemented by the browser, many requirements in this RFC relate to application-level decisions: how the signaling channel is secured, whether HTTPS is used, how user consent is managed, CORS configuration, and how the application handles device permissions. The application's choices in these areas directly affect security.

findings.requirement: It MUST NOT be possible for arbitrary sites to initiate calls to arbitrary locations without user consent. User consent for camera and microphone access is required.

evidence.observation: The application uses navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia() which triggers the browser's built-in permission dialog for camera and microphone access. The user must explicitly grant permission before media devices are accessed. The application also handles graceful degradation if video is denied (falls back to audio-only). Media is only captured after explicit user action (navigating to the room page).

findings.requirement: A script from origin A MUST only be allowed to initiate communications (and hence to access the camera and microphone) if the user has specifically authorized access for that origin.

source_files.code_snippet:
services:
  - type: web
    name: web-conference
    runtime: node
    buildCommand: npm install
    startCommand: node server/index.js
    envVars:
      - key: NODE_ENV
        value: production
      - key: OPENAI_API_KEY
        sync: false

evidence.observation: The application is deployed on Render.com which provides HTTPS by default for all web services. When accessed via HTTPS, the browser enforces origin-based permissions for getUserMedia(). However, the server code creates an HTTP server (not HTTPS) using http.createServer(). In local development without Render's HTTPS termination, the app runs over plain HTTP. While Render handles HTTPS in production, the application itself does not enforce or redirect to HTTPS.
evidence.gap_description: The application server does not implement HTTPS natively or enforce HTTPS redirects. It relies entirely on the deployment platform (Render) for TLS termination. In non-Render deployments or local development, the app runs over HTTP, where origin-based security for device permissions is weakened (some browsers restrict getUserMedia to secure contexts).

findings.remediation: Add HTTPS enforcement at the application level: implement an HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect middleware in Express for production environments. Also consider using helmet.js for additional security headers.

findings.standard_section: 4.1.4 Security Properties of the Calling Page
findings.requirement: The calling page should be served over HTTPS to prevent network attackers from injecting malicious code that could access the camera/microphone using previously granted permissions.

source_files.code_snippet:
const express = require('express');
const http = require('http');

services:
  - type: web
    name: web-conference
    runtime: node
    buildCommand: npm install

evidence.observation: Production deployment on Render provides HTTPS. However, the application server itself uses http.createServer() and has no native TLS support, no HSTS headers, and no HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect. If deployed outside of Render (or any reverse proxy providing TLS), the application would serve content over plain HTTP, exposing it to the attack described in Section 4.1.4 where a network attacker injects an IFRAME to the calling service origin.
evidence.gap_description: No application-level HTTPS enforcement. No HSTS header. No redirect from HTTP to HTTPS. The application depends entirely on the hosting platform for TLS.

findings.remediation: 1. Add HSTS headers via middleware (e.g., helmet). 2. Add HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect middleware for production. 3. Consider adding native HTTPS support using the 'https' module with certificates for non-platform deployments.

findings.requirement: Until consent has been verified for a given endpoint, traffic other than the consent handshake MUST NOT be sent to that endpoint. ICE provides this consent verification.

evidence.observation: The browser's WebRTC implementation enforces ICE consent verification before sending any media. RTCPeerConnection automatically performs ICE connectivity checks before allowing media flow. The application does not bypass or circumvent this mechanism. Media tracks are added to the connection but data only flows after ICE succeeds.

findings.requirement: STUN transaction IDs must be generated by the browser and MUST NOT be made available to the initiating script, even via a diagnostic interface.

evidence.observation: The application accesses ICE candidates through the standard onicecandidate event, which provides candidate transport addresses but not STUN transaction IDs. The RTCIceCandidate object does not expose transaction IDs. The application does not attempt to access STUN internals or transaction IDs through any diagnostic API.

findings.requirement: Either side may wish to hide their location from the other side entirely by forcing all traffic through a TURN server. Implementations may wish to suppress ICE negotiation until the callee has answered.

evidence.observation: The application has no mechanism for IP address privacy. There is no TURN server configured (required for relay-only mode), no iceTransportPolicy option, and ICE negotiation begins immediately upon joining a room without waiting for user confirmation. Users' IP addresses (both local and server-reflexive) are revealed to all peers as soon as they join a room.
evidence.gap_description: Users have no option to hide their IP addresses from peers. ICE begins automatically upon room join. No TURN-only mode is available. This is particularly concerning for scenarios where a user may want to participate anonymously or protect their network location.

findings.remediation: 1. Add TURN server support. 2. Provide a 'privacy mode' option that sets iceTransportPolicy: 'relay'. 3. Consider delaying ICE candidate exchange until the user explicitly confirms they want to connect to a specific peer.

findings.requirement: It MUST be possible for the communicating parties to establish a channel which is secure against both message recovery and message modification. This service must be provided for both data and voice/video.

evidence.observation: WebRTC RTCPeerConnection provides DTLS-SRTP encrypted media channels by default. All voice and video traffic is encrypted and authenticated. The browser enforces this; the application cannot disable it. The signaling channel uses Socket.IO which, in production on Render, operates over WSS (WebSocket Secure) via HTTPS.

findings.requirement: An automatic, public key-based key exchange mechanism is imperative for WebRTC, and this mechanism SHOULD provide Forward Secrecy (FS). The system MUST NOT provide any APIs to extract long-term keying material or directly access stored traffic keys.

evidence.observation: DTLS-SRTP provides automatic key exchange with Forward Secrecy (using ECDHE). The browser does not expose SRTP keying material to JavaScript. However, the application does not use any identity verification mechanism (IdP, certificate pinning) to detect man-in-the-middle attacks by the signaling server. The signaling server relays SDP offers/answers and could theoretically modify fingerprint attributes to perform a MITM attack.
evidence.gap_description: While DTLS provides forward secrecy and the browser protects keying material, the application has no mechanism to detect if the signaling server is performing a man-in-the-middle attack on the DTLS fingerprints.

findings.remediation: Consider implementing out-of-band fingerprint verification or displaying DTLS fingerprints in the UI for manual verification by security-conscious users.

findings.standard_section: 3.3 Bypassing SOP: CORS, WebSockets, and Consent to Communicate
findings.requirement: Cross-origin access mechanisms (CORS, WebSockets) require consent verification to prevent scripts from sending arbitrary data to another origin.

evidence.observation: The Socket.IO server is configured with CORS origin: '*', which allows any origin to connect to the signaling server. This means any malicious website could connect to the signaling WebSocket and potentially: (1) join rooms and spy on room metadata, (2) inject signaling messages, (3) attempt to manipulate call setup. While WebRTC media itself is peer-to-peer and encrypted, the signaling channel is the control plane for establishing calls, and leaving it open to all origins is a significant security vulnerability.
evidence.gap_description: CORS is configured to allow all origins (origin: '*') for the Socket.IO signaling server. This violates the principle that cross-origin access should be restricted to trusted origins. Any website can connect to the signaling server.

findings.remediation: Restrict the CORS origin to the application's actual domain(s). For production: origin: 'https://your-app.onrender.com'. For development, use an environment variable to configure allowed origins. Example: origin: process.env.ALLOWED_ORIGINS?.split(',') || 'http://localhost:3000'.

findings.standard_section: 4.1.2 Calling Scenarios and User Expectations - Signaling Authentication
findings.requirement: Users should be able to verify who they are communicating with. The calling service should authenticate users before allowing them to participate in calls.

evidence.observation: The application has no authentication mechanism whatsoever. Users self-assign a display name stored in sessionStorage. There is no login, no password, no session tokens, no server-side user verification. Anyone who knows (or guesses) a room ID can join any room. The server-side join-room handler accepts any username without verification. Room IDs are predictable (3 groups of 4 alphanumeric characters).
evidence.gap_description: Complete absence of user authentication. Any person can join any room by knowing/guessing the room ID. Display names are self-reported and not verified. There is no access control on rooms. Room IDs have low entropy and can be brute-forced.

findings.remediation: 1. Implement user authentication (e.g., JWT tokens, OAuth, or simple password-protected rooms). 2. Add room access control (e.g., room passwords, invite-only rooms, host approval for joining). 3. Increase room ID entropy to prevent guessing. 4. Validate usernames server-side. 5. Consider implementing Socket.IO middleware for authentication.

findings.requirement: Protecting against active attacks during a call requires positive authentication of the remote endpoint, such as explicit out-of-band key verification or a third-party identity service.

evidence.observation: The signaling server relays SDP offers and answers without any integrity protection or identity binding. A compromised or malicious signaling server could modify the SDP fingerprint attributes to perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the DTLS-SRTP connection. The application does not implement any identity provider (IdP) mechanism, SDP integrity verification, or out-of-band fingerprint checking.
evidence.gap_description: No mechanism exists to verify peer identity or detect man-in-the-middle attacks by the signaling server. SDP messages are relayed without integrity protection. No IdP integration, no certificate pinning, no fingerprint display for manual verification.

findings.remediation: 1. As a minimum, display DTLS fingerprints in the UI to allow security-conscious users to verify them out-of-band. 2. Consider implementing WebRTC Identity Provider (IdP) integration. 3. Add SDP integrity checking or signing.

findings.requirement: Browsers need to provide mechanisms to reset persistent identifiers (DTLS certificates, RTCP CNAMEs) with the same lifetime as cookies. The API should provide mechanisms to force minting of fresh identifiers for anonymous calling.

evidence.observation: This requirement is directed at browser implementations, not applications. The application does not manage DTLS certificates or RTCP CNAMEs. Modern browsers generate per-origin or per-session DTLS certificates for WebRTC, and use privacy-preserving CNAME generation per RFC 7022. The application does not reuse key pairs across sessions (each new RTCPeerConnection gets fresh keying material).

findings.requirement: Screen/application sharing has higher security implications than camera/microphone access. Users may inadvertently share sensitive information visible on their screen.

evidence.observation: The application uses getDisplayMedia() which triggers the browser's screen sharing permission dialog, which is separate from and more prominent than the camera/microphone dialog. The browser handles the selection of what to share (entire screen, window, or tab). However, the application does not provide any additional UI warnings about screen sharing risks beyond what the browser provides. The application does show a visual indicator when screen sharing is active (button state change).
evidence.gap_description: While browser-level consent is obtained, the application does not provide additional warnings about the risks of screen sharing (e.g., accidentally sharing sensitive windows, email, banking sites).

findings.remediation: Consider adding an application-level warning dialog before initiating screen share, reminding users to close sensitive windows/tabs. Provide a clear, persistent indicator that screen sharing is active.

findings.requirement: The Same-Origin Policy prevents server A from mounting attacks on server B via the user's browser. Cross-origin interactions should be mutually consensual.

evidence.observation: The wildcard CORS policy combined with the absence of authentication means any website can embed a script that connects to this signaling server and joins rooms. This could be exploited by a malicious site to: (1) enumerate active rooms, (2) inject fake participants, (3) attempt to disrupt calls, or (4) collect metadata about users (usernames, room IDs). The Socket.IO connection does not require any origin verification, authentication token, or API key.
evidence.gap_description: The combination of CORS origin: '*' and no authentication creates an open signaling server that any website can interact with, violating the Same-Origin Policy protections that WebRTC's security model relies upon.

findings.remediation: 1. Restrict CORS to the application's domain(s). 2. Add Socket.IO authentication middleware. 3. Implement CSRF protection for the API endpoints. 4. Add rate limiting to prevent abuse.

findings.requirement: WebRTC MUST use ICE for consent verification. Non-ICE candidates are not acceptable for WebRTC.

evidence.observation: ICE is mandatory in WebRTC and is implemented by the browser. The application uses standard RTCPeerConnection which always uses ICE. There is no way for the application to disable ICE in the WebRTC API.

recommendations.title: Restrict CORS to trusted origins
recommendations.description: Change Socket.IO CORS configuration from origin: '*' to the application's actual production domain(s). This prevents arbitrary websites from connecting to the signaling server. Use environment variables to configure allowed origins per environment (development vs. production). Example: origin: process.env.CORS_ORIGIN || 'http://localhost:3000'.

recommendations.title: Implement user authentication and room access control
recommendations.description: Add authentication to prevent unauthorized room access. At minimum, implement: (1) room passwords or invite links with tokens, (2) server-side validation of join requests, (3) Socket.IO middleware that verifies authentication tokens. Consider OAuth or JWT-based authentication for more robust security. Also increase room ID entropy to prevent guessing attacks.

recommendations.title: Enforce HTTPS at the application level
recommendations.description: Add Express middleware to redirect HTTP to HTTPS in production and set HSTS headers. Use the helmet npm package for comprehensive security headers. Ensure the application cannot be accessed over plain HTTP in production.

recommendations.title: Add IP address privacy controls with TURN support
recommendations.description: Deploy TURN servers and provide users with a privacy mode option that forces relay-only ICE (iceTransportPolicy: 'relay'). This prevents peers from learning the user's IP address, which is important for privacy-sensitive use cases.

recommendations.title: Add peer identity verification mechanism
recommendations.description: Implement at least basic peer identity verification. Display DTLS certificate fingerprints in the UI so that security-conscious users can verify them out-of-band (similar to Signal's safety numbers). Consider integrating a WebRTC Identity Provider (IdP) for automated identity verification in federated scenarios.

recommendations.title: Enhance screen sharing warnings
recommendations.description: Add an application-level confirmation dialog before starting screen share that warns users about the risks of accidentally sharing sensitive content. Ensure there is a clear, persistent visual indicator when screen sharing is active.
